sentences.text: I want to ask-- I want to ask [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] [INTERPOSING VOICES] [INAUDIBLE] [INTERPOSING VOICES] [INAUDIBLE] At last--
Elizabeth Feldman-de Jong.
I'm born in Holland, Amsterdam.
There were four children, and my mother and my father had four kids.
The oldest one was Sophia, and the second was Frederika, and I was the third, as Elizabeth.
And then I had a brother, Joseph.
Well, I was born in-- in 1916 in December.
And my both parents were deaf.
They were born deaf, and we-- we spoke all of us on our hands.
Mostly on our hands.
Yeah, the sign language, so with our parents, we mostly-- they had a little bit of voice, but mostly they were deaf.
They both went to school in Groningen.
And my mother was in Groningen 18 years.
That was like an institution.
They slept there.
Groningen was it was the deaf institution.
It was in Groningen.
I know the name is Guyot.
In Groningen.
It was in-- in like a province for the Netherlands.
And it was a big, big deaf institution-- I talk about long time ago.
And the kids went only to-- my mother was for 18 years and my father.
And the-- it was actually my mother was six or seven years old.
My mother is from a deaf family altogether.
Her parents were deaf.
Her two brothers were deaf, the whole family.
And my father, it was not.

My father had like a meningitis and brain-- the-- the-- the-- the membrane of the brain was-- so when he was very-- he was only three or four years old when he was-- he was from a normal family, like speaking.
So when my father was three or four years old, he was very sick.
And then after the big fever, his hearing was gone.
And they-- so my father helped my mother's brothers.
So when my mother was 14 or 15 years old, my father was already 25, and he fell in love with my mother.
So he waited seven years until she came out of school and then married her.
Yeah, after-- I was born in Amsterdam.
All-- all of us-- my sister, my brother, everybody was born in Amsterdam.
So after my mother finished school in Groningen, and she came to Amsterdam, married my father, and then, you know, they got four children.
Yeah, and it was good-- good childhood.
No.
I had-- my-- so, my parents were deaf, I told you.
And for them to go in a synagogue, so they wouldn't understand it anyway.
They knew all the Jewish laws.
My mother-- I don't-- I never asked where she got it from.
Maybe from her mother.
She told us all the stories.
See, my mother was a great storyteller, and my grandmother was living in our place, too.
My mother's mother was living also in Amsterdam.
In our home.
And they had like-- so they always were telling about Joseph and Moses and the little-- and they had an enormous-- they couldn't speak very well, but had an enormous theatrical way to telling things.
So as a child, I was already very, very-- knew all the stories, actually more the stories than-- than actually the religion.
We know we were Jewish, but we never never went in a shul.
They were also a little bit socialist.
My parents are a socialist.
At that time-- In what way?
When I was young-- don't forget I was born in 1916.
And that-- the-- the-- the socialists came and worked-- the people should work eight hours a day, eight hours a day, and the social-- kids should not supposed to go to work.
Kids should go to school until they were 14 at that time.
And it was only 48 hours a week you're supposed to work, not 80 hours a week.
I talk for a long time ago.
So my parents were strong believers in-- in a new-- new world.
That they should be--
No, they-- no they were not active at all.
My mother was very well educated, well, after all, they were so many years in Groningen, and she could read and write very well.
And you love to read.
Newspapers and books.
In that time, they start library-- used to come there.
But they are very heavy on kids to be educated, you know?
And my mother was a very strong believer in that you should be-- go to school and learn well.
And, you know, and she did a beautiful job even when she was deaf.
I have to say my father was a diamond cutter, but at that time when I grew up, it was a lot of people were without work.
Like now.
It was a very, very bad situation.
Not much work, and my mother was sewing-- my mother, making money for-- too.
Yeah, she was a good seamstress, very well.
Who could make fur, who could everything.
My father was a diamond cutter, but many times, there was no work.
When the rest-- if the-- the first thing that people don't buy is diamonds in that time.
So there was a lot of times no work.
So my-- and there was no-- no social assistance at that time.
How much was maybe, maybe one guilder or two guilders, nothing.
So my mother had to work to keep us going.
And-- I-- I can remember when I come down, she was working in the night to make money.
My father was helping her.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very warm family.
See, my mother couldn't hear.
And was not very well disturbed by other people.
Her whole life was her family.
Around her brothers, and you know, and what-- she was a member from the deaf society.
They-- they visit each other.
It was like a place they had play yards and they read and made little play things like stage play.
It was all pantomimes, but they-- they were really-- my mother did a lot of those things.
How did you learn to sign?
Now, the signing I did automatically, when I saw my father and my mother signing all the time.
But speaking, I had two older sisters, but I have one sister who was six years older than I was.
And the second was five years older than I was.
So I was already quite-- my sisters were always talking.
So I-- I knew talking and also sign language you get-- I did it automatically.
And I was in a-- in a highchair, I used to tell my mother the bell rings, you know, or-- or the things we had-- all things that we automatically did.
And my mother was very warm, very in tune, very close to us.
I told you my mother was always-- I told you, my mother was always in-- in a deaf society.
And they were very-- all the deaf people came together, and they did things together.
They make plays, they make-- played cards, they, you know, and they talked and they made coffee.
There was a little coffee shop there.
And I really always, always came there.
I was really very much involved in the deaf-- deaf society.
Though we could hear, but we still-- for me, it didn't make any difference.
I was-- like a little thing.
I was very talkative when I was young, very talkative.
Talked a blue streak.
So my mother went to me in the streetcar, and used to say to me, well, you know, if you talk always on your hands, everybody stares, stares at you what you're doing.
And then my mother used to say to me before to me say, listen-- they called me [?
Bepi ?].
I'm Elizabeth.
She said, I don't want you to talk to me when we go in the streetcar.
We go out of the streetcar, you talk to me again.
In the streetcar, you're quiet.
Well, I only talked with my mother on my hands and big, you know, big signs and things like this.
And everybody was staring.
So my mother said, don't talk.
I-- I-- was no sooner than a minute in the streetcar, I forgot all about it.
I didn't know the reason why I couldn't talk to my mother, you know?
And then my mother said, shh, shh, you know, don't talk.
But I did not listen.
For me, it was so normal.
It was a natural way of life.
A natural way-- and I did that, I did-- still talk to her, and then we came out of the streetcar, everybody would say to my mother, who is deaf, you or the child?
And my mother used to say, no.
It's me.
And they say, oh, better that way.
[LAUGHS] You know, [INAUDIBLE] My mother said to me, do not talk to me you know.
But I was so-- tell her stories and things-- we were very close and were very-- I had actually a very nice youth.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No.
My mother had only two brothers, and I had also nephews-- two, three nephews, who were deaf, born deaf.
But my mother had four kids who could hear, but my mother was-- my father was not deaf born.
So the-- and my father had a big family.
My father had about five sisters, I think, and two brothers.
But my father was the youngest of family.
So I can remember my aunts and uncles that I had to go Friday night visit them, and they-- they were quite well-to-do people, my father's side.
They lived in Amsterdam?
They lived in Amsterdam, in Antwerp, but mostly in Amsterdam.
My father had two sisters, and they were-- they had a big doll factory.
My father had porcelain and they did fairly young.
They had a big factory and were quite well-to-do.
So Fridays, we had some dinner, we had to visit them.
I remember things, that had to get a big [INAUDIBLE] before I-- you know, when we were small and very-- have to behave myself, you know?
Things like this I remember from my father.
They could hear.
They were not deaf.
[INAUDIBLE] Can you tell me a little bit about going to school, growing up in Amsterdam?
When I-- when I was, well, six years old, every kids go to school, and I went just to public school, six years, yeah, till I-- till I was-- that's only six years.
And the seven and the and eight and the nine it was actually in higher school.
There must be a Jewish community, but I have to tell you the truth.
I never associated with it.
I have to tell another thing.
The first time-- it's maybe not nice to say-- I was in a synagogue, it was in Canada.
Yeah, because we-- in Holland, we were quite assimilated.
In Holland, the-- the Dutch Jewish people in Holland lived there for a long time.
Like for my husband-- he's from Portugal, they came here 1400, 1500, with the Spanish Inquisition, he came in Holland.
They were there for 300, 400 years.
So my-- my-- my-- my-- my-- like my-- my grandmother and my great-grandmother, what I can remember, they were all Dutch.
They did not-- maybe far, far, far away they came maybe from Russia.
I know that my-- my mother's family, they-- they came later.
No, actually my husband's-- no.
But my mother came here.
My-- my grandmother was born here.
Here in--
In Holland.
And I think her mother was even from-- was born in-- in-- in Holland.
I-- I go-- they go back a long time, and they were quite assimilated, though they knew they were Jewish.
But-- and there was also a Jewish district in Holland, but we never lived there.
Well, we did not specially want to live in-- in-- I don't think they did it on purpose.
It just happened that way.
So I had girlfriends and-- and they were Gentile and Jewish.
And it was not a big deal.
Oh, definitely.
Not when I was a child.
None.
Not that I can remember.
Well, this was already 1939.
I was-- I got married in 1936.
And my husband-- I show you later picture-- my first husband, Max, his father, he was an artist.
We have paintings here.
He was an artist, a sculpture.
And I got married with him in 1936.
And so then I moved from Amsterdam to Haamstede.
It was a little place out of-- out of Amsterdam, quite a bit out-- Haamstede, very close to the coastline.
And we had a nice house there, 10 rooms.
And he painted, and I-- I went to work.
I was a designer-- then already.
I had my diploma for designing, and I went off to work.
And we did not know children at all.
We did-- actually, my husband was always against it.
He didn't want children.
So that was till-- till the war came.
We heard already about the war when we were grown up.
Like, when-- when Hitler came in power, I was not a child.
I was 27 years when I went to Auschwitz.
So it was not as a long time.
We knew--
First of all-- first of-- We knew about talking about it and you know, and-- then the Germans fled Germany, went to Holland.
That was 1937, I think, 1938, before-- before the war.
So we knew about it, that the Jewish were-- and the-- the Kristallnacht that they went, you know-- we know about the papers and you know, we heard about it.
So we were all very shocked.
And-- and my brother-- actually my sister and her husband-- they had German like, Jewish people in-- in their house.
They took them, but then they visit us, and we know that there were razzias already in Holland and there was-- Did you hear anything about concentration camps or anything like that?
No, we knew that there was concentration-- that they-- listen, if the razzias, the people were sent out, you heard about they went to camps and it was very bad.
And they talked about killings.
But who would believe it?
Yeah, we did try when-- the war started in 1940, when Germany came in and you heard about and first they came to Poland.
Then you were already on-- then they came to Holland.
They were already on the march then, and we know how much razzias were there for Jewish people.
They picked them up, and they sent them away.
It was already very bad.

So when I lived in Haamstede still, and we know that the war started in 1940-- and we-- when I stayed in Haamstede in my garden, I saw-- I heard and saw the people flying over the Rotterdam and that-- that they captured Holland, you know, they came-- the Germans came in Holland.
And he made Rotterdam nearly flat.
They bombarded.
And Holland is very small.
It's not a big country.
It's populated, but it's not-- small.
So the Germans came in, and we were very, very sad for sure, that-- What happened when they came in?
What did you see?
Now, we know that-- that-- that-- that-- was bombarded-- the bombarded.
And-- and the Dutch government, like the queen and her-- her cabinet and everybody, her people, they fled to England.
So Holland was basically without, without a head of state, nothing.
So when Germans came in Holland, they took over.
And then they started-- like the laws were already in Germany-- they came to in Holland.
Like you had to wear a star, that you have to give your radio in, that you can't go shopping after five o'clock.
That was also a very, very traumatic experience.
First, I told you I was living in Holland, like-- like wild-- you know, wild?
We were just like everybody else.
We were not singled out that we were Jewish and I was into-- I told you I was in a sport organization.
I was in a youth organization.
I did a lot of, you know, we were all active in-- in-- in other things-- sports and things like this and sailing and-- Name the youth organization that you belonged to.
The-- the-- the first one was the-- the ISC.
That is the-- the-- the arbiters, like the workers youth center-- center.
And it was for working people, like you came together, and we went on walking things, and we-- we did all kinds of things.
And music, we played mandolin and guitar, folks.
Social group.
And all were the same.
And it was-- there was all Jewish and-- and Gentile.
It was not specific Jewish.
I was in the-- No, I did not in the youth organization whenever I went to Haamstede.
But we did go swimming at the [INAUDIBLE].
It's also like-- there was a youth sport group that was not the-- the-- the other group.
So we were swimming and-- and all kinds of sports we did.
Mostly he painted.
Mostly he made a book-- like layouts, books, things, and--
Painting, no.
He painted in the war, too, but he was sensitive about it happened.
He was very-- he knew [INAUDIBLE].
He knew-- he was very, very afraid that time that they told the people would get picked up in razzias and things like that.
He made a lot of friends in Haamstede.
Haamstede was a small place.
It was maybe two or three Jewish families.
And the most mine friends were Gentile.
That was in 19-- first came out, they were lost that we couldn't go to-- it was also a very traumatic experience.
Can you remember, I swam every day and suddenly big signs outside, Jewish not-- Jewish can't go in.
Oh, yeah, right away, the star.
And-- and we couldn't do shopping before five o'clock.
We couldn't do the radio.
We couldn't go bicycle.
We couldn't go anything.
So suddenly, I felt isolated.
Between all those people, I think gee, I am different.
I am not-- I am not [?
Bepi ?] anymore.
I'm just an outcast.
I felt very bad, yeah.
And it was a funny feeling.
Well, well, you know, grow up.
I was already 25 or 26.
And everything is free, and suddenly you come in a-- in a-- in a environment that you-- that just-- that you-- that you're outcast, you know?
You're an absolute outcast.
And I couldn't believe.
It couldn't have happened.
I felt very bad, but the people around me were nice.
They came in, and they gave me food, and they tried to do things, you know?
Emptied out in-- in the next day already.
Came with the big tractors and took everything out.
So I had neighbors I was very good with, and I had a piano.
I had a piano in my house, and I-- I played a little bit.
And I said to her, I said-- if you come in-- we go-- we have to go away, maybe tonight you to take the piano.
It was a good one, a Steinway.
I said, and you take them out and then you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No, I didn't.
I don't-- we didn't know.
We didn't think about anything.
We had to take-- we-- my parents lived already in the ghetto.
They lived in Amsterdam.
They moved them out of the place, and gave them another place to live in the-- they call it the ghetto.
It was like a couple streets.
They called it the ghetto, actually.
And so we were only two days by my mother in-- in-- in Amsterdam and the Dutch police, who was already-- they were still good.
They was not overtaken by the SS's.
And we had a lot-- we had also [INAUDIBLE] in Holland, a lot of people, sympathizers, Nazi sympathizers who were police, I think.
And those Dutch police took us in the night out of my mother's place, and I said, you go there and there are friends of ours.
So soon we came in a house, they said right away, they said, oh, yeah.
You can stay with us.

Oh, yeah, when we were still in Haamstede, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when-- when we were still in Haamstede, we had-- we had people who were-- said why don't you take some false papers and maybe you can-- you don't have to-- we had a big star on-- the Jewish star on our passport.
And they made us false papers without-- without-- a false name and also no star on it.
But I was still afraid to go.
Everybody knew me in Haamstede.
I was still afraid.
I never used them.
And then-- and then we-- I went into hiding.
Over a year and a half.

First, we went to our-- the grocery-- the guy from the grocery store who always had a horse and a wagon, who came out and was talking to us, and said-- but he had five kids, and he had another friend.
And he said, I have another friend who has no children, and they want to take you in.
He said, I'll bring you there.
The kids know you.
The kids knew me.
It was in Haarlem.
Oh, yeah.
It was in their home.
Yeah, we were in an attic.
And it was a room in the-- on the-- on the-- on the-- on the top floor.
And those people were fantastic.
Enormous good people.
No.

The people were-- while we were there, I think my brother-in-law, who was-- who was married to my oldest-- my oldest sister didn't even want to go in hiding before-- she couldn't hide-- hide before me.
She didn't want to go in hiding before all her family was safe.
She was very, very conscious about it.
My mother and my father should be in hiding and everything.
Sophia.
She-- she-- they-- they-- I think they paid some, but very little.
But my parents who were still in Amsterdam, the SS came all the time to my parents, and told them, listen, what happened to your daughter and your son-in-law.
See, my mother, who couldn't hear, you know, and said, all the time, I don't know where they are.
I don't know.
So they was-- they were very afraid that they would pick them up.
They were in hiding with me.
There was in-- in hiding place were my father, my mother, my-- my husband, and me.
Four, at least four of us in the small attic for year and a half.
We didn't go out of the-- you couldn't go on the street.
Nobody's supposed to know that the people were-- and it was very danger to hide people.
Yeah, he was working, and she was cooking for us and doing things for us.
You had to be very careful.
There was only one washroom downstairs.
My father couldn't hear and my mother.
So that nobody came in-- they had to stay in the washroom until we told them they can go out.
Yeah, we-- we had-- at that time already, there-- there was people who were-- the [INAUDIBLE] started to work already.
Don't forget it was 1942, and there was a lot of things in Holland and razzias and picking up and people that sent--
